edges.description: Reduced alpha-globin production leads to excess unpaired beta-globin chains that form unstable HbH tetramers (beta4).
edges.target: Beta-Globin Chain Excess and Hemoglobin H Formation

edges.description: Reduced alpha-globin synthesis directly lowers hemoglobinization and produces microcytic hypochromic anemia.
Impaired hemoglobin synthesis produces microcytosis with decreased MCV.
Loss of all four alpha-globin genes produces Hb Bart syndrome with severe fetal anemia and hydrops fetalis.
HbH inclusion bodies cause oxidative membrane damage and shortened red cell survival.
edges.source: Beta-Globin Chain Excess and Hemoglobin H Formation

edges.description: Iron released from hemolyzed red cells contributes to iron loading.
HbH inclusion body membrane damage produces chronic hemolytic anemia.
Chronic hemolysis increases bilirubin production and causes jaundice.
Chronic hemolysis predisposes to pigment gallstones.
Chronic red-cell destruction and compensatory hematopoiesis enlarge the spleen.
Severe anemia, especially in Hb Bart syndrome, can induce congestive heart failure.
Ineffective erythropoiesis suppresses hepcidin, increasing intestinal iron absorption.
Ineffective marrow erythropoiesis drives compensatory hematopoiesis outside the marrow.
Chronic erythroid marrow expansion produces thalassemia-like craniofacial bone changes.
Extramedullary erythropoiesis and iron loading contribute to liver enlargement.

nodes.description: In the absence of sufficient alpha-globin chains, excess beta-globin chains form unstable tetramers called hemoglobin H (beta4). HbH precipitates as inclusion bodies in mature red cells, causing membrane damage and premature destruction. In the most severe form (Hb Bart syndrome), excess gamma-globin chains form hemoglobin Bart (gamma4), which has extremely high oxygen affinity and cannot deliver oxygen to tissues.
nodes.id: Beta-Globin Chain Excess and Hemoglobin H Formation

evidence.snippet: varying degrees of ineffective erythropoiesis, decreased red blood cell (RBC) survival, chronic hemolytic anemia, and subsequent comorbidities

nodes.description: Pigment gallstones from chronic bilirubin overproduction secondary to hemolysis.

evidence.snippet: may develop gallstones and experience acute episodes of hemolysis

nodes.description: Peripheral destruction of red blood cells containing HbH inclusions. In HbH disease, hemolysis is typically chronic and compensated but may become acutely exacerbated by infections, oxidant drugs, or pregnancy. In Hb Bart syndrome, the anemia is severe and incompatible with extrauterine life without intervention.

evidence.snippet: may develop gallstones and experience acute episodes of hemolysis in response to infections or exposure to oxidant drugs

nodes.description: Severe anemia-induced heart failure in Hb Bart syndrome. May also occur in severe non-deletional HbH disease or with chronic iron overload.

evidence.snippet: congestive heart failure induced by severe anemia

nodes.description: Low MCV is the hallmark laboratory finding in alpha-thalassemia trait and HbH disease. Often the initial finding prompting further evaluation.

evidence.snippet: An intermediate form of alpha-thalassemia characterized by increased hemolysis and mild to severe anemia with marked microcytosis and hypochromia.

nodes.description: Deletions or point mutations in HBA1 and/or HBA2 genes reduce or abolish alpha-globin chain production. Large deletions are the most common cause, removing one or both alpha-globin genes on chromosome 16p13.3. Non-deletional mutations (point mutations, small insertions/deletions) tend to produce more severe phenotypes for the same number of affected alleles.

evidence.snippet: characterized by decreased synthesis of α-globin chains that results in an imbalance of α and β globin
biallelic pathogenic variants in both HBA1 and HBA2 that result in deletion or inactivation of all four

nodes.description: Compensatory hematopoiesis in liver, spleen, and other sites. Marked in Hb Bart syndrome and severe HbH disease.

evidence.snippet: Extramedullary erythropoiesis, marked hepatosplenomegaly, and a massive placenta are common
biallelic pathogenic variants in both HBA1 and HBA2 that result in deletion or inactivation of all four
biallelic pathogenic variants in HBA1 and HBA2 that result in deletion or inactivation of three

nodes.description: Chronic hemolysis from HbH inclusion body-mediated membrane damage. Acute exacerbations triggered by infections or oxidant drugs.

evidence.snippet: experience acute episodes of hemolysis in response to infections or exposure to oxidant drugs

nodes.description: Liver enlargement from extramedullary hematopoiesis and iron deposition. Less common than splenomegaly.

evidence.snippet: enlargement of the spleen (and less commonly of the liver)

nodes.description: Generalized edema with pleural and pericardial effusions due to severe fetal anemia in Hb Bart syndrome (--/--). The hallmark of the most severe form of alpha-thalassemia.

evidence.snippet: characterized by prenatal onset of generalized edema and pleural and pericardial effusions as a result of congestive heart failure induced by severe anemia

nodes.description: Reduced hemoglobin synthesis leads to small, pale red blood cells. Severity ranges from absent (silent carriers) to profound (Hb Bart syndrome). HbH disease typically shows moderate anemia with Hb 7-10 g/dL.

evidence.snippet: ranging from a silent or mild carrier state to severe, transfusion-dependent or lethal disease

nodes.description: Expanded but ineffective erythroid compartment in bone marrow. Erythroid precursors are damaged by precipitated globin chains, leading to intramedullary apoptosis. More prominent in non-deletional HbH disease and Hb Bart syndrome.

evidence.snippet: disease modifying agents that target ineffective erythropoiesis and chronic hemolytic anemia

nodes.description: Increased serum ferritin and tissue iron deposition, particularly in transfusion-dependent patients or those with non-deletional HbH disease.
Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia from chronic hemolysis. Typically mild in HbH disease.

evidence.snippet: mild jaundice, and sometimes thalassemia-like bone changes

nodes.description: Splenic enlargement from extramedullary hematopoiesis and increased erythrocyte destruction. Present in most individuals with HbH disease.

evidence.snippet: The majority of individuals have enlargement of the spleen (and less commonly of the liver), mild jaundice, and sometimes thalassemia-like bone changes

nodes.description: Skeletal changes from marrow expansion, including frontal bossing and maxillary overgrowth. Seen in more severe forms of HbH disease.
